title: The uneven toll of NIH grant cuts
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Neanderthals’ precarious existence, the ongoing cooperation of pregnancy, and more
Brought to you by Danaher Corporation
View this email in your browser
24 March 2026
Today’s Protostar is Dakota McCoy, one of the two inaugural winners of the Chrysalis Prize. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including Neanderthals’ precarious existence and the gendered effects of NIH grant cuts.
PHysiology  |  News from Science
Hormone linked to morning sickness may help reduce alcohol intake
What happens if you drink around 7 liters of beer a day for 3 days at Oktoberfest, Munich’s famous autumn beer festival? Among other things, your levels of a hormone linked to morning sickness may go up, a Danish research team has found. In a preprint published this month, they argue that the hormone, GDF15, may signal to the body it’s time to cut back.

GDF15 rises sharply during early pregnancy and is thought to contribute to vomiting and feelings of sickness, perhaps to reduce the fetus’ exposure to unfamiliar or spoiled food. It’s also been linked to appetite suppression in people who aren’t pregnant.

In the new study, researchers saw a rise—albeit not statistically significant—in GDF15 levels among three people who partook in the German festival. They also turned up data from the UK Biobank showing that people carrying a mutation that disables a receptor for GDF15, thus hampering the hormone’s role, drank more per week than non-carriers. People with alcohol dependence, meanwhile, had GDF15 levels about five times higher than healthy individuals, perhaps reflecting desensitization to the hormone’s alcohol-limiting role.

More work is needed to establish GDF15’s effects, the researchers acknowledge, but they hope the hormone might eventually point the way to a treatment for alcohol dependence.
Read the full story
Ancient DNA  |  News from Science
Neanderthals lived their whole existence on a knife’s edge
New genetic research shows Neanderthal groups were small and isolated.  Aleksei Gorodenkov/Alamy
Between 400,000 and 45,000 years ago, Neanderthals living across Eurasia hunted large game, harvested plants, expertly knapped stone tools, and fashioned clothing from animal skins. In many ways, they were well-suited to their environment. Yet their existence was always precarious . Two new studies show many Neanderthals lived in small, far-flung groups, likely experienced significant inbreeding, and survived a close brush with extinction about 75,000 years ago before bouncing back to repopulate the continent—and then finally winking out.

These latest studies both rely on ancient DNA to tell their tales. In one, researchers found that Neanderthals accumulated a lot of genetic variation over relatively short spans of time —a pattern usually seen in heavily inbred populations. Even so, the genetic impact of such inbreeding appears to have been small for hundreds of thousands of years, provided the climate proved relatively stable. But once they encountered a major climate shift, that lack of genetic diversity may have proved a fatal hindrance, researchers speculate.

The second study details a near-extinction event for Neanderthals some 75,000 years ago. Ancient DNA and archaeological patterns suggest that at that time, Neanderthal populations plummeted to maybe a few thousand individuals total, again perhaps due to a shifting climate. The survivors holed up in the valleys of southern France, while abandoning or dying out in the rest of the continent. Then, they bounced back, at least for a while.

It’s unclear what exactly allowed our lineage to prevail while Neanderthals ultimately went extinct, but these studies offer clues that could one day help solve that mystery.
Read the full story
Scientific Funding  |  PNAS
Uneven toll of NIH grant cuts
Cuts to National Institutes of Health grants in 2025 hit women researchers, especially those early in their career, harder than male investigators, according to an analysis published this week in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

The authors dug into nearly 2300 NIH awards, worth $2.45 billion, that were axed either because they involved a topic President Donald Trump’s administration considered off-limits, or because of alleged antisemitism at the grantee’s university. Women’s research grants were on average two-thirds as large as men’s, making it harder for them to weather a funding disruption, the authors suggest. And women had more money left in their grants than men when they were cancelled; on average 42% versus 52% of the total. Although women made up only 38% of professors who lost funding, they made up 60% of the affected assistant professors and doctoral students.

“These harms were predictable,” Jenna Norton, a program director at the NIH’s National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases, told Fierce Biotech. “In fact, they were predicted by many staff inside NIH, but our warnings were ignored.”

In a statement to STAT, an NIH spokesperson noted that the NIH allocated its full budget in 2025, “providing funding to scientists at all stages of their careers who are performing high-impact science,” adding that “NIH supports a fair and objective review process that evaluates proposals based on scientific merit. Any other suggestion is false.”
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Danaher combines AI-driven discovery with a proven strategic framework to expand what's possible for the future of healthcare.
Read Now
Protostar
Courtesy of Dakota McCoy
Dakota McCoy
Assistant Professor, University of Chicago and the Marine Biological Laboratory
Dakota McCoy has a knack for finding hidden conflicts in apparent harmony. As an assistant professor at the University of Chicago and the Marine Biological Laboratory, McCoy uses the tools of evolutionary biology, biophysics, and game theory to study some of nature’s most intimate partnerships. To most people, coral reefs and mammalian pregnancies look like seamless cooperation. To McCoy, they are ongoing negotiations, shaped by millions of years of competing genetic interests.

This spring, the Cromwell Harbor Foundation named McCoy one of two inaugural recipients of its Chrysalis Prize, awarding her $250,000 in unrestricted support for early-career scholars doing unusually imaginative, cross-disciplinary work.

The thread connecting McCoy’s seemingly disparate projects is a preoccupation with what happens when two genetically distinct individuals share one body. In coral, a reef-building animal hosts single-celled algae inside its own cells; during bleaching events, that partnership catastrophically dissolves. In pregnancy, a mother gestates an embryo that shares only half her genes. McCoy thinks the fragility of both relationships—the severity of coral bleaching and the alarming rate of pregnancy complications—stems from the same underlying dynamic: these are evolved compromises, not perfectly engineered solutions.

ScienceAdviser spoke with McCoy about her work. Below is that conversation, edited for brevity.

At first glance, coral reefs and human pregnancy don’t have much in common. What do you see that others might miss?
In nature, you have different genetic individuals living together in these extremely close, intimate symbioses. One of the most famous is the coral reef, where the coral animal houses algae—these single-celled plants—inside their cells, and they somehow make that relationship work. The physics of nutrient transfer, the physics of getting the sunlight where it needs to go for photosynthesis, that interesting dialog supports the entire ecosystem of coral reefs. But during coral bleaching events, that symbiotic relationship goes bad. My general theory is that one reason why it breaks down so catastrophically is that it’s not as mutualistic as you think.

And then the second big, close, intimate symbiosis that interests me is mammalian pregnancy. Just like a coral hosts a totally different genetic individual—the algae—inside itself, mothers sort of unbelievably evolved the ability to gestate an alien embryo inside their body—someone that’s only 50% related to them. The hormones that evolved during pregnancy create very interesting game-theoretical dynamics between the interests of the embryo and the evolved genetic interests of the mother.

While we think of pregnancy as a beautiful miracle that’s cooperative—and it totally is—it’s also an evolved negotiation between the interests of the alien genes in the mother and the mother’s genes. And that returns me to the theme: Why is human pregnancy so risky? Something like one in five pregnancies, even with good health care, has a severe complication. I think because, just like the coral reef, it’s an evolved compromise.

Where does game theory come in?
Game theory comes into play as a way to model the evolution of genes over time. The placenta and the fetus produce certain hormones in massive, massive quantities. If this were totally cooperative, you’d expect very little genetic change over time. But instead, what you see in certain cases is massive, rapid selection, which seems to be operating like an arms race.

Or take coral: When you have a coral with many, many individual symbiont cells inside its body, you can think of that as a public goods problem. The coral provides things like nitrogen and phosphorus to fertilize the algae, and the algae produce sugars through photosynthesis. So, you can imagine algae evolving to be selfish and choosing a strategy of defect: take the public good nutrients but not provide enough photosynthesis. That would be a winning strategy, if you can go undetected. My students found that algae do seem to adopt many different strategies. They’re not perfectly controlled, which raises interesting questions. But the main idea is that you can think about biological systems as marketplaces where nutrients and goods are exchanged with different prices and different market failures.

Most people think of coral reefs and human pregnancy as cooperative mutualism. And they absolutely are in many ways. But there are these subtle conflicts of interest that crop up—and they are where a lot of the strangest dynamics play out, like rapid, accelerating coral bleaching, or really scary and severe complications in pregnancy. If we take these ideas from game theory, I think we can actually better characterize and better predict what’s going to happen, and better design interventions to potentially help protect the reef and help make pregnancy safer. Those are the long-term applied goals.

You’ve mentioned that you yourself are pregnant. Does that change how you think about this research?
I’ve been interested in pregnancy since long before I became pregnant. It’s a very understudied topic, particularly through the lenses of evolutionary biology and biophysics, so there’s just a lot of open questions there. But now that I myself have been pregnant and all my friends are thinking about or trying to become pregnant, I see firsthand just how uncertain it is to try to conceive and raise a little baby. Anything can happen at any stage of the process. I understand just how fragile it is, and how grateful we should be.

From an evolutionary perspective, you’d expect pregnancy would work better. Just like my heart and lungs work quite well because they evolved roughly with one purpose—to help me, in my body, win the Darwinian game. But pregnancy is an evolved compromise and maybe it doesn’t work better because of that.
Read about the winners
Et Cetera
Bombing history
At least 56 cultural sites, monuments, and museums in Iran have been damaged since U.S. and Israeli attacks began on 28 February. “Most of these towns that we’re bombing are filled with historic houses and a lot of other cultural heritage that flies under the radar,” one expert said.
Read more at ScienceInsider
New Lyme vaccine misses bullseye
Pfizer and Valneva announced in a press release that four doses of its experimental vaccine for Lyme disease showed greater than 70% efficacy in preventing the debilitating condition in a randomized, controlled trial of more than 9000 people. But there were so few cases of Lyme among the trial participants overall that the result wasn’t statistically significant at the prespecified confidence level required to declare it a success.
Read more at News from Science
An 11-year hot streak
According to the World Meteorological Organization’s State of the Global Climate report 2025, 2015 to 2025 were the hottest 11 years on record. 2025 itself was the second or third hottest year. “We seem to be entering this new era where temperatures will be significantly higher than what they were 10 years ago,” one climate scientist noted. 
WMO report  |  Read more at Nature
14%
The percentage of nearly 9500 cities tested that met the World Health Organization’s annual average PM 2.5 guideline limit of 5 µg/m³ in 2025
Data pulled from 9446 cities in 143 countries, regions, and territories indicate that wildfires and climate change are intensifying global air pollution.
2025 IQAir World Air Quality Report
Last but not least
Today, I am impressed by this little spider’s fungal costume—such dedication to cosplay!
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Martin Enserink, Michael Price, Jocelyn Kaiser, and Ana Georgescu

raw_metadata.subject: The uneven toll of NIH grant cuts
